categories.summary: A large multicenter study found that early patterns in six vital signs split critically ill ischemic stroke patients into three distinct physiologic "phenotypes" with very different mortality risks, and these trajectory-based groups predicted one‑year outcomes better than standard ICU scores—opening the door to more personalized fluid and sedative strategies at the bedside. A small 12‑week trial in people with primarily progressive multiple sclerosis showed that repeated intermittent hypoxia (simulated high‑altitude sessions) was safe, well tolerated, and hinted at functional benefits, supporting larger controlled trials of this potential neuroprotective therapy. And a U.S. Medicaid claims analysis revealed that women with epilepsy were slightly more likely to be prescribed contraception, women with intellectual and developmental disabilities were much less likely, and those with both conditions fell in between—pointing to inequities in reproductive care that need tailored attention.
